perturbation.original_text: For a period of 3 years from the date of disclosure, Recipient agrees to use the same care and discretion as it uses with its own c onfidential information to avoid, except as expressly permitted in this Agreement, disclosure or publication of Discloser’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: For a period of 1 year from the date of disclosure, Recipient agrees to use the same care and discretion as it uses with its own c onfidential information to avoid, except as expressly permitted in this Agreement, disclosure or publication of Discloser’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under Utah law, potentially violating the reasonable measures required to maintain trade secret status.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Utah Uniform Trade Secrets Act, Utah Code Ann. § 13-24-2
perturbation.law_explanation: The Utah Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy.  A one-year confidentiality period for potentially long-lived trade secrets might not be considered a reasonable effort, thus potentially jeopardizing trade secret protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Utah Code § 13-24-2 (2023) - Definitions. :: 2023 Utah Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Utah Code 2023 Utah Code (here) 2022 Utah Code View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2023 Utah Code Title 13 - Commerce and Trade Chapter 24 - Uniform Trade Secrets Act Section 2 - Definitions. Universal Citation: UT Code § 13-24-2 (2023) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 13-24-2. Definitions. As used in this chapter, unless the context requires otherwise: (1) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) "Trade secret" me
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period from 3 years to 1 year. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation state that a one-year confidentiality period may not be sufficient to protect trade secrets under Utah law, potentially violating the 'reasonable efforts' standard required to maintain trade secret status. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'misappropriation,' which includes the disclosure or use of a trade secret without consent. A shorter confidentiality period increases the risk of misappropriation, and if deemed unreasonable, could jeopardize trade secret protection under Utah law.

perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement for its convenience upon wri tten notice to the other.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement for its convenience without any prior notice to the other.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the written notice requirement for termination contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Utah contract law, which requires reasonable notice.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing, recognized under Utah Law (see cases such as Rawson v. Conover, 2001 UT 24, 20 P.3d 876)
perturbation.law_explanation: Utah law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract.  Terminating a contract without any prior notice could be seen as a breach of this covenant, especially if it causes undue hardship to the other party.
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the requirement for written notice, potentially contradicting the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. The Law Explanation states that terminating a contract without any prior notice could breach this covenant, especially if it causes undue hardship. The original text required written notice, while the changed text removes that requirement entirely, potentially causing undue hardship and violating the implied covenant. This falls under the definition of Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall begin on the date it is executed and shall extend until that date which is six (6) months after the date on which Individual ceases to be a participant in TTP.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall begin on the date it is executed and shall extend indefinitely, regardless of whether Individual ceases to be a participant in TTP.
perturbation.explanation: Making the agreement extend indefinitely, even after the individual leaves TTP, creates an unreasonable restriction that is against public policy and the right to work under Utah law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The right to work is protected in Utah.  An indefinite non-disclosure agreement, especially one that continues even after the relevant individual is no longer participating in the activity, could be seen as an unreasonable restraint on trade and the individual's ability to seek employment, thus contradicting public policy and potentially violating the spirit of Article I, Section 1 of the Utah Constitution regarding inherent and inalienable rights.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Constitution of Utah - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia (Redirected from Utah Constitution ) Governing document of the State of Utah Overview Jurisdiction Utah Created 1895 Presented 1895 Ratified 1896 Government structure Branches 3 Chambers Bicameral Executive Governor The Constitution of the State of Utah defines the basic form and operation of state government in Utah. [ 1 ] History [ edit ] Utahns had drafted seven previous constitutions starting in 1849 as part of repeated attempts to become a state. [ 2 ] In 1850, the federal government created the Utah territory, with a secular government. Utah was still prohibited from joining the union, due to the practice of polygamy in Utah. In 1890, The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints renounced polygamy , which gave Utah permission from the federal government to begin drafting a constitution. [ 3 ] The Utah Constitution was drafted at a convention that opened on March 4, 1895 in Salt Lake City . The constitution was later approved by the citizens of Utah . It took several attempts to get a constitution approved by Congress, which admitted Utah as a state in 1896. [ 2 ] Rights enshrined in the Utah Constitution [ edit ] Beginning with Hansen v. Owens , 619 P.2d 315 (Utah 1980), the Utah Supreme Court embarked upon a short-lived venture during which the court interpreted Article I, § 12 of the Utah Constitution as providing greater protection against self-incrimination than that which is provided by the Fifth Amendment . The Hansen decision was based upon the unique language of Article I, § 12, which speaks in terms being compelled "to give evidence against [one]self" rather than being compelled "to be a witness against [one]self." A mere five years later the court retreated from this position and in American Fork City v. Crosgrove , 701 P.2d 1069 (Utah 1985), overruled Hansen . This, however, did not put an end to the notion that the Utah Constitution may provide greater protection than does the federal Bill of Rights . It is now clear that Article I, § 14 of the Utah Constitution provides greater protection to the privacy of the home and automobiles than does the Fourth Amendment . [ 4 ] The expansion of the protection afforded by the state constitution has not been based upon distinctions in the language used, nor has it been the result of Utah's unique political and religious h
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the agreement indefinitely, regardless of the individual's participation in TTP. This contradicts the right to work by potentially imposing an unreasonable restraint on trade, as explained in the LLM Law Explanation. The Utah Constitution, as described in Scraped Law Snippet 2, defines the basic form and operation of state government in Utah, including rights. An indefinite agreement could violate the spirit of these rights, particularly if it hinders an individual's ability to seek employment after leaving TTP, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In any legal proceeding arising out of this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to an award of its costs and reasonabl e attorneys' fees.
perturbation.changed_text: In any legal proceeding arising out of this Agreement, the losing party shall pay a penalty of $10,000 to the prevailing party, regardless of costs and reasonable attorneys' fees.
perturbation.explanation: Imposing a fixed penalty of $10,000 regardless of actual damages or attorney fees may be considered an unenforceable penalty clause under Utah contract law, especially if it is disproportionate to the actual harm suffered.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Unenforceable Penalty Clause, Utah Law (see cases such as Woodhaven Apartments v. Washington, 942 P.2d 918 (Utah 1997))
perturbation.law_explanation: Utah law disfavors penalty clauses in contracts. A fixed penalty that is not reasonably related to the actual damages incurred as a result of the breach is likely to be deemed an unenforceable penalty. The new term sets an arbitrary payment, regardless of the actual damages suffered or costs incurred by the prevailing party.
perturbation.justification: The original text allows for costs and attorney's fees to be awarded to the prevailing party. The changed text imposes a fixed penalty of $10,000 to be paid to the prevailing party regardless of actual costs and fees. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both state that this may be considered an unenforceable penalty clause under Utah contract law if the penalty is disproportionate to the actual harm suffered, which contradicts the principle that damages should be related to actual losses. The lack of scraped law snippets does not invalidate this contradiction, as the explanations provide a clear basis for it.
